  • • It is an Ultra-Short acting Barbiturate
  • • true
  • • ultra-short acting barbiturate
  • • very rapid onset of action
  • • short duration of effect
  • • Option B . It has a Short Duration of Action due to Rapid Redistribution : - This statement is true . Thiopentone's short duration of action is primarily due to its rapid redistribution from the brain to other tissues in the body.
  • • Option B
  • • It has a Short Duration of Action due to Rapid Redistribution
  • • true
  • • short duration of action
  • • rapid redistribution
  • • brain to other tissues
  • • Option D . All of the following statements are True : -This statement is not true because Statement C is incorrect .
  • • Option D
  • • All of the following statements are True
  • • not true
  • • Statement C is incorrect

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Thiopentone is an ultra-short acting barbiturate with a short duration of action due to rapid redistribution . It tends to cause a decrease in blood pressure due to vasodilation , not a marked rise in blood pressure.

It reduces intracranial pressure by decreasing cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) flow and facilitating its absorption . It also reduces cerebral blood flow and cerebral blood volume , which collectively help lower intracranial pressure .

  • • Option B . Thiopentone has a Rapid Onset of Action : Thiopentone has a rapid onset of action due to its high unionized fraction and high lipid solubility . This allows the drug to cross the blood-brain barrier quickly and induce anesthesia promptly .
  • • Option B
  • • Thiopentone has a Rapid Onset of Action
  • • rapid onset
  • • action
  • • high unionized fraction
  • • high lipid solubility
  • • cross the blood-brain barrier quickly
  • • induce anesthesia promptly

  • • concern in patients with head injuries

Educational Objective:

question: Dissociative anesthesia is seen with which Intravenous Induction Agent:

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 35-year-old female with suicidal tendencies is posted for Electro-Convulsive Therapy. (ECT). Which of these is the drug of choice for Electro-Convulsive Therapy?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Methohexital

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • A. Ketamine: While ketamine has been used in ECT due to its antidepressant properties, it is not the drug of choice because it tends to raise blood pressure and heart rate, which may not be desirable in all patients undergoing ECT.
  • • B. Propofol: This is a commonly used induction agent in anesthesia that has anti-convulsant properties. Its use in ECT might be counterproductive as it could increase the seizure threshold and make it harder to induce the therapeutic seizure.
  • • C. Thiopentone: Similar to propofol, thiopentone is an anesthetic agent with anti-convulsant properties, making it less favorable for ECT where a seizure is the intended outcome. While it can be used, it is not preferred over methohexital, which is more pro-convulsant.

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Methohexital is the preferred drug for ECT because it lowers the seizure threshold , facilitating the induction of seizures , which are a key component of the therapy.
  • ➤ preferred drug for ECT

  • ➤ facilitating the induction of seizures
  • ➤ key component

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of the following statements is not true regarding Thiopentone?
options: Induction Dose is 3-5 mg/kg of 2.5% Thiopentone.
It Reduces ICP and is Cerebroprotective
It is Ultra-Short Acting due to Rapid Metabolism &amp; Excretion.

explanation.short: C. It is Ultra-Short Acting due to Rapid Metabolism & Excretion.
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. It is Ultra-Short Acting due to Rapid Metabolism & Excretion.

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Option A . Induction Dose is 3-5 mg/kg of 2.5% Thiopentone : This statement is true . The typical induction dose of Thiopentone is 3-5 mg/kg of a 2.5% solution . The dose may vary depending on the patient's age, weight, and specific clinical circumstances .
  • • Option A
  • • Induction Dose is 3-5 mg/kg of 2.5% Thiopentone
  • • true
  • • 3-5 mg/kg of a 2.5% solution
  • • patient's age, weight,
  • • specific clinical circumstances
  • • Option B . It Reduces ICP and is Cerebroprotective : This statement is true . Thiopentone is considered cerebroprotective because it Reduces Intra-Cranial Pressure (ICP) by depressing cerebral metabolism . It can be used in patients with raised ICP and seizures to help manage intracranial hypertension .
  • • Option B
  • • It Reduces ICP and is Cerebroprotective
  • • true
  • • cerebroprotective
  • • Reduces Intra-Cranial Pressure (ICP)
  • • depressing cerebral metabolism
  • • raised ICP
  • • seizures
  • • manage intracranial hypertension
  • • Option D . Intra Intra-Arterial Injection cause Pain & Vasospasm : This statement is true . Intra-arterial injection of Thiopentone is associated with pain at the injection site and vasospasm , which can lead to adverse effects , including potential ischemia in the affected limb .
  • • Option D
  • • Intra Intra-Arterial Injection cause Pain & Vasospasm
  • • true
  • • pain at the injection site
  • • vasospasm
  • • adverse effects
  • • potential ischemia
  • • affected limb

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective
  • ➤ Thiopentone is an ultra-short-acting intravenous induction agent primarily due to rapid redistribution , not rapid metabolism and excretion.

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of these drugs are included in premedication prior to surgery?

Explanation:

  • • Explanation.

  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Option A . Midazolam : Midazolam is a benzodiazepine commonly used for premedication before surgery . It has anxiolytic (anxiety-reducing) and sedative properties , making it useful in calming anxious patients before entering the operating room. It can also induce anterograde amnesia , which helps patients forget the events immediately before and during surgery .

  • • Option B. Granisetron : Granisetron is a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist that is often included in premedication to prevent postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV ). PONV is a common complication following surgery, and drugs like granisetron help reduce the risk of these unpleasant symptoms.
  • • Option B.
  • • Granisetron
  • • 5-HT3 receptor antagonist
  • • postoperative nausea
  • • vomiting (PONV
  • • common complication
  • • granisetron
  • • reduce the risk
  • • Option C. Pantoprazole : Pantoprazole is a Proton Pump Inhibitor (PPI) used to reduce gastric acid secretion . It is included in premedication to decrease the risk of aspiration pneumonia , especially in patients who may have a full stomach at the time of surgery . By reducing stomach acid production , pantoprazole helps prevent gastric contents from entering the lungs and causing pneumonia .

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of these intravenous induction agents is preferred in cardiac surgeries?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Etomidate

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • In cardiac surgeries , Etomidate is often the preferred intravenous induction agent . This is because Etomidate is the most Cardio-Stable ( Hemodynamically Stable ) among the intravenous induction agents . It causes the least hemodynamic disturbance , making it suitable for patients with cardiac disease , such as heart failure , valve dysfunction , or those undergoing aneurysm , bypass , or valve surgery .

  • • bypass
  • • valve surgery
  • • Other intravenous induction agents like Propofol , Thiopental , and Barbiturates can cause dose-dependent vasodilation and hypotension , which may not be well-tolerated in patients with compromised cardiac function.
  • • Propofol
  • • Thiopental
  • • Barbiturates
  • • dose-dependent vasodilation
  • • hypotension

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:

question: For the first case of the day you open a vial of propofol. 10 ml of propofol is remaining in the vial after end of the case. You wish to reuse it for the second case. Propofol vial, once opened, should be used within how many hours?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. 6 hours

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Propofol vials, once opened , should be used within 6 hours . Propofol is an oil-based preparation that contains soybean oil, egg lecithin, and glycerol . The milky white solution can support bacterial growth once the vial is opened . To prevent contamination and ensure patient safety , it is essential to use the propofol solution within the specified time frame .

  • • newer

  • • disodium edetate
  • • sodium metabisulfite
  • • prevent bacterial growth
  • • extend the usability period
  • • after opening

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:

This is due to its stimulatory effects on the central nervous system and the potential for increased cerebral blood flow .

  • • susceptible individuals

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 36-year-old female presented to emergency with cough, fever, and abdominal and back pain for one week. She had tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypotension and bilateral rales on auscultation. Chest radiographs revealed rounded densities in the right middle lung. Initial assessment of the chest radiographs signified acute respiratory failure with severe sepsis, likely secondary to pneumonia. The patient was intubated and placed on mechanical ventilation, started on vasopressors, antibiotics, propofol and midazolam for sedation. Her condition worsened and that resulted in increased doses of all sedative agents and the addition of fentanyl. 2-3 days later, she developed generalized rash over the body along with elevated Creatinine, sinus tachycardia, diastolic dysfunction and metabolic acidosis. What is the likely diagnosis?
options: Respiratory Failure due to Over Sedation

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Propofol Infusion Syndrome

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . Renal Failure due to Sepsis: While sepsis can lead to Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) , the clinical presentation described in the scenario, including a generalized rash, sinus tachycardia, diastolic dysfunction, and metabolic acidosis, is not primarily indicative of renal failure.
  • • Option A
  • • Renal Failure due to Sepsis:
  • • sepsis
  • • Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)
  • • not primarily indicative
  • • Option B . Fentanyl Overdose : While fentanyl is an opioid that can lead to respiratory depression when overdosed , the symptoms mentioned in the scenario, such as a generalized rash and diastolic dysfunction, are not typical of opioid overdose.
  • • Option B
  • • Fentanyl Overdose
  • • fentanyl is an opioid
  • • respiratory depression when overdosed
  • • not typical of opioid overdose.
  • • Option C . Respiratory Failure due to Over Sedation : Over-sedation with propofol, midazolam, and fentanyl can cause respiratory depression , but the additional symptoms like rash, diastolic dysfunction, and metabolic acidosis are not consistent with this diagnosis .
  • • Option C
  • • Respiratory Failure due to Over Sedation
  • • can cause respiratory depression
  • • not consistent with this diagnosis

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Essential features of propofol infusion syndrome are metabolic acidosis , multiple organ failure, hemodynamic instability, hepatomegaly , and rhabdomyolysis . It occurs due to long term propofol infusion (> 48 hours) for sedation at high doses (>5 mg/kg/hr) leading to impaired fatty acid metabolism in the body .
  • ➤ propofol infusion syndrome
  • ➤ metabolic acidosis
  • ➤ multiple organ failure, hemodynamic instability, hepatomegaly
  • ➤ rhabdomyolysis
  • ➤ long term propofol infusion
  • ➤ (> 48 hours) for sedation at high doses (>5 mg/kg/hr)
  • ➤ impaired fatty acid metabolism in the body
  • ➤ Patients will have Green color Urine as Propofol contains Phenol.
  • ➤ Patients will have Green color Urine as Propofol contains Phenol.
  • ➤ 1st sign of Propofol infusion syndrome is Abnormal levels of Lipemia
  • ➤ 1st sign of Propofol infusion syndrome is Abnormal levels of Lipemia

subject: Anesthesia
question: Reverse coronary steal phenomenon is seen with which IV induction agent?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Thiopentone

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Option A . Ketamine: Ketamine typically increases cerebral blood flow due to its sympathomimetic effects , which would not result in a reverse steal phenomenon.
  • • Option A
  • • Ketamine:
  • • increases cerebral blood
  • • flow
  • • sympathomimetic effects
  • • Option B . Propofol : Propofol decreases cerebral blood flow due to its global cerebral vasoconstrictive effects but is not specifically associated with the reverse steal phenomenon.
  • • Option B
  • • Propofol
  • • decreases cerebral blood flow
  • • global cerebral vasoconstrictive effects
  • • Option C . Etomidate : While Etomidate is known for maintaining hemodynamic stability and is used for its minimal effects on cerebral blood flow , it is not associated with the reverse coronary steal phenomenon.
  • • Option C
  • • Etomidate
  • • maintaining hemodynamic stability
  • • minimal effects
  • • cerebral blood flow

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Reverse steal phenomenon also called as Robin hood phenomenon is seen with thiopentone . In this, Thiopentone constricts the cerebral and coronary vasculature , causing a decrease in blood flow, blood volume , and in the brain even intracranial pressure . However, this vasoconstriction occurs only in normal areas . The blood vessels in the ischemic area remain dilated and there is redistribution of blood flow from normal to ischemic areas in the brain. This is called the Robin Hood phenomenon .
  • ➤ Reverse steal phenomenon also called as Robin hood phenomenon is seen with thiopentone .

question: For an upper esophagogastroduodenoscopy, the GI surgeon asks you to give Conscious sedation to the patient. Conscious sedation is best given with:

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Dexmeditomidine

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Option A . Propofol:
  • • Option A
  • • Propofol:
  • • Propofol is a potent sedative-hypnotic agent commonly used for procedural sedation . It provides rapid onset and offset of sedation , making it suitable for short procedures like endoscopy . However, propofol can cause respiratory depression and may require close monitoring of the patient's airway and ventilation . It is not as selective as Dexmedetomidine and can lead to a deeper level of sedation , potentially compromising the patient's ability to maintain arousal .
  • • Propofol is a potent sedative-hypnotic agent commonly used for procedural sedation .

  • • potentially compromising
  • • patient's ability
  • • maintain arousal
  • • Option B . Ketamine:
  • • Option B
  • • Ketamine:
  • • Ketamine is a dissociative anesthetic agent that can provide sedation, analgesia , and amnesia . It has unique properties that make it suitable for some procedures but not ideal for conscious sedation during endoscopy. Ketamine can cause hallucinations , which might be unsettling for patients during the procedure. While it doesn't typically depress respiratory function , it can lead to increased salivation and laryngospasm , which can be problematic during an upper endoscopy . These properties make Dexmedetomidine an excellent choice for conscious sedation during upper endoscopy.

Dexmedetomidine, a highly selective alpha-2 adrenoreceptor agonist , is the preferred choice due to its minimal respiratory and cardiovascular depression , ability to provide good sedation and analgesia , and sympatholytic effects , which reduce patient discomfort and stress during the procedure.

question: A 3-year-old child is posted for upper GI endoscopy. The procedure will take only 15 minutes and the child will get discharged on the same day, so you decide to give ketamine along with a drug that reduces emergence delirium. Which is the most effective drug in preventing emergence delirium with ketamine?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Midazolam

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Option A . Atropine:
  • • Option A
  • • Atropine:
  • • Atropine is an anticholinergic medication that can reduce oral secretions and help prevent excessive salivation during procedures. While it may be useful for certain purposes, it does not have a direct role in preventing emergence delirium , which is a common side effect of ketamine in pediatric patients.
  • • Atropine is an anticholinergic medication that can reduce oral secretions and help prevent excessive salivation during procedures.
  • • anticholinergic medication
  • • reduce oral secretions
  • • prevent excessive salivation
  • • While it may be useful for certain purposes, it does not have a direct role in preventing emergence delirium , which is a common side effect of ketamine in pediatric patients.
  • • does not
  • • direct role
  • • preventing emergence delirium
  • • common side effect of ketamine
  • • Option B . Remifentanil:
  • • Option B
  • • Remifentanil:
  • • Remifentanil is an opioid analgesic that provides pain relief and sedation but does not specifically target emergence delirium associated with ketamine. Opioids may have their own set of side effects in pediatric patients , and they are typically used for analgesia rather than preventing ketamine-induced delirium.
  • • Remifentanil is an opioid analgesic that provides pain relief and sedation but does not specifically target emergence delirium associated with ketamine.
  • • opioid analgesic
  • • pain relief and sedation
  • • Opioids may have their own set of side effects in pediatric patients , and they are typically used for analgesia rather than preventing ketamine-induced delirium.
  • • side effects in pediatric patients
  • • Option C . Thiopentone:
  • • Option C
  • • Thiopentone:
  • • Thiopentone is a short-acting barbiturate that is used as an induction agent for general anesthesia . It does not have a specific role in preventing emergence delirium associated with ketamine.
  • • Thiopentone is a short-acting barbiturate that is used as an induction agent for general anesthesia .
  • • short-acting barbiturate
  • • induction agent
  • • general anesthesia
  • • It does not have a specific role in preventing emergence delirium associated with ketamine.

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ In pediatric patients undergoing procedures involving ketamine, such as an upper GI endoscopy, the prevention of emergence delirium is important for their comfort and well-being . While ketamine can induce delirium upon emergence , administering a benzodiazepine like midazolam prior to ketamine administration is an effective strategy to reduce the occurrence of this phenomenon . Midazolam's sedative and amnestic properties make it the preferred choice among benzodiazepines for this purpose. Additionally, the use of glycopyrrolate intravenously can help reduce oral secretions during the procedure.

  • ➤ preferred choice

  • ➤ reduce oral secretions

subject: Anesthesia
question: Propofol is the most preferred drug for rapid recovery from anesthesia. What is the mechanism of this rapid recovery?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Redistribution

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Option B . Methylation:
  • • Option B
  • • Methylation:
  • • Methylation is a metabolic process that involves the addition of a methyl group to a molecule , and it is not the primary mechanism for the rapid recovery from anesthesia with propofol .
  • • Methylation is a metabolic process that involves the addition of a methyl group to a molecule , and it is not the primary mechanism for the rapid recovery from anesthesia with propofol .
  • • metabolic process
  • • addition
  • • methyl group
  • • molecule
  • • not the primary mechanism
  • • rapid recovery
  • • anesthesia with propofol
  • • Option C . Excretion:
  • • Option C
  • • Excretion:
  • • Excretion refers to the removal of drugs or their metabolites from the body through processes like urine, feces, or exhalation. While excretion plays a role in eliminating drugs from the body, it is not the primary mechanism for the rapid recovery from propofol-induced anesthesia .
  • • Excretion refers to the removal of drugs or their metabolites from the body through processes like urine, feces, or exhalation. While excretion plays a role in eliminating drugs from the body, it is not the primary mechanism for the rapid recovery from propofol-induced anesthesia .
  • • removal of drugs
  • • their metabolites
  • • through processes
  • • excretion plays a role
  • • eliminating drugs
  • • not the primary mechanism
  • • rapid recovery
  • • propofol-induced anesthesia
  • • Option D . Bio-Transformation :
  • • Option D
  • • Bio-Transformation
  • • Biotransformation involves the metabolic conversion of drugs within the body . Propofol does undergo biotransformation to some extent, but it is not the primary mechanism responsible for its rapid recovery from anesthesia .
  • • Biotransformation involves the metabolic conversion of drugs within the body . Propofol does undergo biotransformation to some extent, but it is not the primary mechanism responsible for its rapid recovery from anesthesia .
  • • metabolic conversion
  • • within the body
  • • undergo biotransformation
  • • not the primary mechanism
  • • rapid recovery
  • • anesthesia

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective
  • ➤ The rapid recovery from anesthesia with propofol is primarily attributed to the mechanism of redistribution . After intravenous administration , propofol quickly enters the bloodstream , leading to an induction of anesthesia as it initially accumulates in highly perfused tissues like the brain. As the drug continues to be administered , it redistributes to less perfused tissues , reducing its concentration in the central nervous system . This redistribution process results in the patient waking up from anesthesia within approximately 20 minutes of discontinuing propofol infusion .

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of the following statements is not true regarding total intravenous anesthesia (TIVA)?
options: Most commonly used drug is Propofol
There is less risk of Post-Operative Nausea + Vomiting
It is preferred in Malignant Hyperthermia
Bispectral Index monitor does not need to be used with TIVA

explanation.short: D. Bispectral Index monitor does not need to be used with TIVA
explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Bispectral Index Monitor does not need to be used with TIVA

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Intra-Operative Awareness is a significant problem associated with TIVA . This awareness can be checked by Bispectral Index .
  • • Intra-Operative Awareness
  • • significant problem associated with TIVA
  • • checked by Bispectral Index
  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Let’s evaluate each option:
  • • Option A . Most commonly used drug is Propofol :
  • • Option A
  • • Most commonly used drug is Propofol
  • • This statement is true . Propofol is one of the most commonly used drugs for TIVA . It is preferred for its smooth induction, rapid recovery, and predictable pharmacokinetics .
  • • This statement is true . Propofol is one of the most commonly used drugs for TIVA . It is preferred for its smooth induction, rapid recovery, and predictable pharmacokinetics .
  • • true
  • • most commonly used drugs for TIVA
  • • smooth induction, rapid recovery,
  • • predictable pharmacokinetics
  • • Option B . There is less risk of Post-Operative Nausea + Vomiting :
  • • Option B
  • • There is less risk of Post-Operative Nausea + Vomiting
  • • This statement is generally true . TIVA, when compared to inhalation anesthesia, is associated with a lower incidence of post-operative nausea and vomiting (PONV). This is one of the advantages of TIVA.
  • • This statement is generally true . TIVA, when compared to inhalation anesthesia, is associated with a lower incidence of post-operative nausea and vomiting (PONV). This is one of the advantages of TIVA.
  • • generally true
  • • lower incidence of post-operative nausea and vomiting (PONV).
  • • Option C . It is preferred in Malignant Hyperthermia :
  • • Option C
  • • It is preferred in Malignant Hyperthermia
  • • This statement is true . TIVA is often preferred in patients with a history or risk of malignant hyperthermia ( a rare but life-threatening reaction to certain anesthesia agents ), as it allows for precise control of anesthesia and avoids triggering this condition .
  • • This statement is true . TIVA is often preferred in patients with a history or risk of malignant hyperthermia ( a rare but life-threatening reaction to certain anesthesia agents ), as it allows for precise control of anesthesia and avoids triggering this condition .
  • • true
  • • preferred in patients
  • • a rare but life-threatening reaction to certain anesthesia agents
  • • allows
  • • precise control of anesthesia
  • • avoids triggering this condition

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Total Intravenous Anesthesia (TIVA) is a technique that involves the administration of intravenous anesthetic agents to maintain anesthesia throughout a surgical procedure . While TIVA offers advantages such as smooth induction, rapid recovery , and reduced risk of post-operative nausea and vomiting , it does not eliminate the need for monitoring the depth of anesthesia . The use of a Bispectral Index (BIS) Monitor is common in TIVA to assess the patient's level of consciousness and prevent intra-operative awareness .

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 5-year-old child is posted for outpatient procedure. Inj. Ketamine was used for induction of anesthesia and the patient was conscious but unable to respond the commands. This is due to which mechanism of action of ketamine:

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. All of the above

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . Inhibition of Thalamocortical Pathway :
  • • Option A
  • • Inhibition of Thalamocortical Pathway
  • • Ketamine inhibits the thalamocortical pathway , which is responsible for transmitting sensory information from the thalamus to the cerebral cortex . By inhibiting this pathway , ketamine disrupts the normal processing of sensory information and contributes to the altered state of consciousness seen in dissociative anesthesia .
  • • Ketamine inhibits the thalamocortical pathway , which is responsible for transmitting sensory information from the thalamus to the cerebral cortex .
  • • Ketamine inhibits
  • • thalamocortical pathway
  • • responsible
  • • transmitting sensory information
  • • thalamus to the cerebral cortex
  • • By inhibiting this pathway , ketamine disrupts the normal processing of sensory information and contributes to the altered state of consciousness seen in dissociative anesthesia .
  • • inhibiting this pathway
  • • disrupts the normal processing
  • • sensory information
  • • contributes
  • • altered state of consciousness
  • • dissociative anesthesia
  • • Option B . Stimulation of the Limbic System :
  • • Option B
  • • Stimulation of the Limbic System
  • • Ketamine also stimulates the limbic system , a region of the brain associated with emotions, motivation, and memory . This limbic system activation can lead to vivid dreams, hallucinations , and alterations in perception , contributing to the dissociative state induced by ketamine.
  • • Ketamine also stimulates the limbic system , a region of the brain associated with emotions, motivation, and memory .
  • • stimulates
  • • limbic system
  • • region of the brain
  • • emotions, motivation,
  • • memory
  • • This limbic system activation can lead to vivid dreams, hallucinations , and alterations in perception , contributing to the dissociative state induced by ketamine.
  • • vivid dreams, hallucinations
  • • alterations in perception
  • • dissociative state induced by ketamine.
  • • Option C . NMDA Antagonism :
  • • Option C
  • • NMDA Antagonism
  • • Ketamine is a non-competitive antagonist at the phencyclidine site of the N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor . NMDA receptors are involved in synaptic transmission and play a crucial role in memory and consciousness . By antagonizing these receptors, ketamine disrupts normal NMDA receptor-mediated neurotransmission , leading to a dissociative state where the patient remains conscious but unable to process sensory input or respond to commands .
  • • Ketamine is a non-competitive antagonist at the phencyclidine site of the N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor .
  • • non-competitive antagonist
  • • phencyclidine site
  • • N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor
  • • NMDA receptors are involved in synaptic transmission and play a crucial role in memory and consciousness .
  • • synaptic transmission
  • • crucial role
  • • memory and consciousness
  • • By antagonizing these receptors, ketamine disrupts normal NMDA receptor-mediated neurotransmission , leading to a dissociative state where the patient remains conscious but unable to process sensory input or respond to commands .
  • • disrupts normal NMDA receptor-mediated neurotransmission
  • • dissociative state
  • • patient remains conscious
  • • unable to process sensory input
  • • respond to commands
  • • Educational objective:
  • • Educational objective:
  • • The mechanism of action of Ketamine is by:
  • • mechanism of action of Ketamine

  • • Option B. Ketamine:

  • • Propofol is an intravenous induction agent that is known for its antiemetic properties .
  • • intravenous induction agent

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective
  • ➤ In patients with a history of motion sickness and severe nausea and vomiting, it is important to select anesthesia induction agents that have a lower incidence of postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) . Etomidate, while effective for induction , is associated with a relatively high incidence of PONV . Therefore, it is not the ideal choice for such patients. Drugs like ketamine and propofol, which have antiemetic properties , are often preferred to minimize the risk of PONV and ensure a more comfortable perioperative experience for these individuals.

This technique offers early recovery , a shorter duration of action , and a lower risk of complications compared to other anesthesia methods. The main goal of ambulatory surgery is to minimize the duration of hospital stay while providing adequate surgical anesthesia .

subject: Anesthesia
question: Dr. Soonawalla is preparing to perform a diagnostic procedure on a 70-year-old patient with cardiovascular comorbidities. The procedure involves potential discomfort but is relatively brief and does not require significant surgical intervention. The patient has expressed a wish to be sedated but not completely unconscious, and to have no recall of the procedure. Which type of anesthesia care would be the most suitable in this situation?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC)

Explanation:

  • • Explanation
  • • Option A . Local Anesthesia - This type of anesthesia is often used for minor procedures and numbs a small area of the body.
  • • Option A
  • • minor procedures
  • • numbs a small area
  • • Option B . General Anesthesia - Under general anesthesia, the patient is completely unconscious . This might be more than what is necessary for this patient's relatively brief, non-major procedure . Plus, given the patient's cardiovascular comorbidities , the risks of general anesthesia could be unnecessarily high .
  • • Option B
  • • completely unconscious
  • • patient's relatively brief, non-major procedure
  • • patient's cardiovascular comorbidities
  • • unnecessarily high
  • • Option C . Regional Anesthesia - This type of anesthesia numbs a larger area of the body than local anesthesia but doesn't make the patient unconscious
  • • Option C
  • • numbs a larger area
  • • doesn't make
  • • patient unconscious
  • • Educational objective
  • • Educational objective
  • • For brief diagnostic procedures requiring sedation without complete unconsciousness , especially in patients with cardiovascular comorbidities , Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC) is the most suitable anesthesia care type to ensure comfort, manage pain , and prevent procedure recall while minimizing risk .

subject: Anesthesia
question: Who is commonly referred to as the "Father of Modern Anesthesia"?

explanation.detail:
Ans. A) William T. G. Morton

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option B. James Simpson:
  • • Option B. James Simpson:
  • • Sir James Young Simpson (1811-1870) was a Scottish obstetrician and physician known for his significant contributions to the field of anesthesia. His most notable achievements include: Discovery of Chloroform as an Anesthetic : James Simpson is renowned for introducing chloroform as an effective and safer anesthetic agent for medical procedures. In 1847, he and his colleagues began experimenting with chloroform's use for pain relief during childbirth and surgeries . This discovery had a profound impact on reducing pain and suffering during medical procedures and childbirth. Advocacy for Anesthesia in Obstetrics : Simpson was a strong advocate for the use of anesthesia, particularly chloroform, during childbirth. His advocacy led to widespread acceptance of anesthesia in obstetrics, transforming childbirth from a painful and often traumatic experience into a more comfortable and manageable one for women.
  • • Sir James Young Simpson (1811-1870) was a Scottish obstetrician and physician known for his significant contributions to the field of anesthesia. His most notable achievements include:

This discovery had a profound impact on reducing pain and suffering during medical procedures and childbirth.

  • • Advocacy for Anesthesia in Obstetrics
  • • Option C. William McEwan:
  • • Option C. William McEwan:
  • • William McEwan (1848-1924) was a Scottish surgeon and a pioneer in the field of anesthesiology. His contributions include: Development of the Portable Ether Inhaler : McEwan is credited with inventing a portable ether inhaler in 1876 . This device allowed for safer and more controlled administration of ether anesthesia during surgical procedures . It was a significant improvement over previous methods of ether administration, making surgery safer and more manageable.
  • • William McEwan (1848-1924) was a Scottish surgeon and a pioneer in the field of anesthesiology. His contributions include:

His notable contributions include: Investigation of Ether and Chloroform : Snow conducted extensive research on the use of ether and chloroform as anesthetics. He published several papers on the administration and safety of these agents, contributing to their widespread adoption in medical practice. Application of Ether in Surgery : Snow was one of the first to apply ether anesthesia in surgery . His work helped establish the safety and effectiveness of ether as an anesthetic for various surgical procedures. Father of Modern Epidemiology : While Snow is primarily known for his contributions to anesthesia, he is also recognized as the "father of modern epidemiology." He made groundbreaking discoveries related to the transmission of cholera , which laid the foundation for the field of epidemiology .

subject: Anesthesia
question: In Guedel's classification of the stages of anesthesia, during which stage is surgery typically performed?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Stage III

Explanation:

  • • Explanation
  • • Option A. Stage I , also known as the stage of analgesia , is the period between the beginning of anesthesia and the loss of consciousness . The patient is awake but feels no pain . However, surgical procedures can't be performed during this stage because the level of anesthesia is not deep enough .
  • • Option A.
  • • Stage I
  • • stage of analgesia
  • • between the beginning of anesthesia
  • • loss of consciousness
  • • awake
  • • feels no pain
  • • surgical procedures can't be performed
  • • not deep enough
  • • Option B . Stage II , known as the stage of excitement or delirium , is characterized by the patient's loss of consciousness, irregular breathing, increased heart rate, and involuntary movements . This is a dangerous stage because of the risk of patient injury and uncontrolled movements , hence, surgery is not performed in this stage.
  • • Option B
  • • Stage II
  • • stage of excitement or delirium
  • • patient's loss of consciousness, irregular breathing, increased heart rate,
  • • involuntary movements
  • • dangerous stage
  • • risk of patient injury
  • • uncontrolled movements
  • • surgery is not performed
  • • Option D . Stage IV , the stage of overdose , is a dangerous stage characterized by severe brain depression leading to cessation of respiration and loss of cardiovascular control mechanisms . If not quickly corrected, it can lead to death , hence it's a stage to avoid during anesthesia administration.
  • • Option D
  • • Stage IV
  • • stage of overdose
  • • dangerous stage

question: Local anaesthetics have greatest affinity for which type of sodium channels?

  • • Sodium channels can exist in an activated state when they are open , allowing the influx of sodium ions during an action potential . Local anesthetics do interact with sodium channels , but their binding affinity is not highest when the channels are in the activated state .

  • • Option C

  • • Sodium channels in the resting state (closed) are not the primary target for local anesthetics. These channels are not actively involved in the generation of action potentials , and local anesthetics are more effective at blocking channels in the inactivated state.
  • • Sodium channels in the resting state (closed) are not the primary target for local anesthetics.

  • • not the primary target

question: Which of these is the main action of local anaesthetics?

explanation.detail:

Explanation:

  • • Option B . NMDA Antagonism :
  • • Option B
  • • NMDA Antagonism
  • • While local anesthetics primarily act on sodium channels, some may have additional actions, such as NMDA receptor antagonism. NMDA receptors are involved in the perception of pain and the transmission of pain signals in the central nervous system . NMDA antagonism may contribute to the analgesic effects of certain local anesthetics .
  • • While local anesthetics primarily act on sodium channels, some may have additional actions, such as NMDA receptor antagonism.
  • • NMDA receptors are involved in the perception of pain and the transmission of pain signals in the central nervous system .

  • • NMDA antagonism may contribute to the analgesic effects of certain local anesthetics .
  • • analgesic effects
  • • certain local anesthetics
  • • Option C . Potassium Channel Blockade:
  • • Option C
  • • Potassium Channel Blockade:
  • • Local anesthetics do not typically block potassium channels as their primary mode of action. The primary target is sodium channels.
  • • Local anesthetics do not typically block potassium channels as their primary mode of action. The primary target is sodium channels.

  • • Option D . Calcium Channel Blockade:

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ The main action of local anesthetics is the blockade of voltage-gated sodium channels in nerves. This blockade prevents the generation and propagation of action potentials , resulting in reversible loss of sensation in the affected area . While some local anesthetics may have additional actions, such as NMDA antagonism, their primary mechanism of action is sodium channel blockade.

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 35-year-old male is posted for lower limb debridement surgery under femoral and sciatic block. The drug used for blockade is 2% lignocaine with adrenaline. Addition of adrenaline to local anesthetic causes?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. Decreased Absorption of LA

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • The addition of adrenaline (epinephrine) to a local anesthetic causes the following effect:
  • • addition of adrenaline (epinephrine)
  • • local anesthetic
  • • Option B .
  • • Option B
  • • Adrenaline , when added to a local anesthetic solution, acts as a vasoconstrictor . Vasoconstrictors like adrenaline reduce the blood flow to the area where the local anesthetic is injected. This decreased blood flow results in reduced absorption of the local anesthetic into the bloodstream , as it limits the rate at which the drug enters the systemic circulation . As a result, the presence of adrenaline in the local anesthetic solution prolongs the duration of action and provides a more profound block , making the anesthesia more effective and longer lasting . This vasoconstrictor action of adrenaline is particularly useful in regional anesthesia techniques like femoral and sciatic blocks , where it helps improve the depth and duration of anesthesia . Vasoconstrictors should be used cautiously and are contraindicated in specific situations, such as injections close to end arteries ( e.g., ring blocks of finger and toe digits ), intravenous regional anesthesia (Bier's block)

  • • Option A . Hypercapnia:
  • • Option A
  • • Hypercapnia:
  • • Local anesthetics have a negative chronotropic ( heart rate-reducing ) and ionotropic (contractility-reducing) effect on the heart. In the presence of hypercapnia (elevated CO 2 ) , the cardio-depressant effects of local anesthetics may be potentiated . To prevent toxicity , it may be necessary to reduce the dose of local anesthetics in patients with hypercapnia .

  • • To prevent toxicity , it may be necessary to reduce the dose of local anesthetics in patients with hypercapnia .

  • • Option B . Hypoxia:
  • • Option B

  • • Similar to hypercapnia, local anesthetics have negative chronotropic and ionotropic effects on the heart . In the presence of hypoxia ( low oxygen ), the cardiodepressant effects of local anesthetics may be potentiated . To prevent toxicity , it may be necessary to reduce the dose of local anesthetics in patients with hypoxia .

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which statement(s) is/are accurate concerning local anesthetics? Prilocaine is Longer Acting than Bupivacaine. Tetracaine is more Potent than Lignocaine. Dibucaine is the Longest Acting Local Anesthetic. Bupivacaine can produce Cardiotoxicity. Cocaine can produce Hypotension.

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. 2,3,4

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Prilocaine is Longer Acting than Bupivacaine: Bupivacaine is known for its long duration of action , while prilocaine has a shorter duration of action compared to bupivacaine. Therefore, this statement is false . Tetracaine is more Potent than Lignocaine: Tetracaine is considered a high-potency local anesthetic and is generally more potent than lignocaine (lidocaine) , which is an intermediate-potency local anesthetic . Dibucaine is the Longest-Acting Local Anesthetic: Dibucaine is known for its very long duration of action among local anesthetics , making it one of the longest-acting agents in this class. Bupivacaine can produce Cardiotoxicity: Bupivacaine, particularly when used in high doses or inadvertently administered systemically , can produce cardiotoxicity , including potential for cardiac arrhythmias and toxicity . Cocaine can produce Hypotension: Cocaine, as a vasoconstrictor, can lead to hypertension rather than hypotension due to its effects on the cardiovascular system .

  • • pKa of Lignocaine is 7.8
  • • pKa of a drug is the pH at which the drug is 50% ionized and 50% non-ionized . Unionized form of local anesthetics penetrates the lipid soluble membranes and acts quickly . More the unionized form faster the onset of action . Hence, pKa correlates with the onset of action . Local anesthetics with a pKa closest to physiological pH will have (at physiological pH) a greater fraction of nonionized base that more readily permeates the nerve cell membrane , generally facilitating a more rapid onset of action . subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of these is not an advantage of vasoconstrictors when added to local anesthetic solutions?
options: Increased Peak Plasma Concentration of Local Anesthetic

explanation.short: C. Increased Peak Plasma Concentration of Local Anesthetic
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Increased Peak Plasma Concentration of Local Anesthetic

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . Increased Duration of Action: This is an advantage of vasoconstrictors when added to local anesthetic solutions . Vasoconstrictors like adrenaline (epinephrine) can constrict blood vessels at the injection site, which reduces the rate of systemic absorption of the local anesthetic. This prolongs the duration of action of the anesthetic, making it effective for a longer period .

  • • Option B . Increased Potency of Blockade : This is also an advantage of vasoconstrictors. Vasoconstrictors not only prolong the duration of action but can also enhance the potency of the local blockade by concentrating the anesthetic at the site of action , leading to more profound anesthesia .
  • • Option B
  • • Increased Potency of Blockade
  • • advantage
  • • not only prolong
  • • also enhance
  • • potency
  • • local blockade
  • • concentrating
  • • anesthetic
  • • site of action
  • • more profound anesthesia
  • • Option D . Marker for Accidental Intravascular Injection : This is another advantage of vasoconstrictors. If a vasoconstrictor like adrenaline is accidentally injected into a blood vessel ( Intravascular Injection ), it can lead to an increase in heart rate and blood pressure , serving as a marker for the inadvertent injection .
  • • Option D
  • • Marker for Accidental Intravascular Injection
  • • another advantage
  • • accidentally injected
  • • blood vessel
  • • Intravascular Injection
  • • lead

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:

However, it does not increase the peak plasma concentration of the local anesthetic; instead, it reduces it , which is important for minimizing the risk of local anesthesia systemic toxicity .

subject: Anesthesia
question: A first year anesthesia resident is giving axillary block by landmark guided technique. She accidentally punctures the axillary artery during the process and injects 20 ml of local anesthetic intravascularly. The patient starts getting seizures bradyarrythmias and severe hypotension within 5 minutes of this injection. What should be the treatment of this condition:

explanation.detail:
Ans. C. 20% Intralipid

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . Dantrolene Sodium : Dantrolene Sodium is a medication used to treat malignant hyperthermia .
  • • Option A
  • • Dantrolene Sodium
  • • medication
  • • treat malignant hyperthermia
  • • Option B . 10% Intralipid : Intralipid, a Lipid Emulsion , is used to treat LAST . However, 10% Intralipid is less concentrated than 20% Intralipid and may be less effective in rapidly reversing the toxic effects of intravascular local anesthetic injection .
  • • Option B
  • • 10% Intralipid
  • • Lipid Emulsion
  • • treat LAST
  • • may be less effective
  • • rapidly reversing
  • • toxic effects

  • • Option D
  • • Vasopressin
  • • treating hypotension
  • • no role in LAST

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective
  • ➤ Local Anesthesia Systemic Toxicity (LAST) can occur when local anesthetics are inadvertently injected intravascularly . The most effective treatment for LAST is the administration of 20% Intralipid , a lipid emulsion , which acts as a lipid sink to sequester and reduce the toxic effects of local anesthetics. Early recognition and prompt administration of 20% Intralipid can reverse the symptoms of LAST and prevent serious complications .

subject: Anesthesia
question: As per the susceptibility of nerve fibres to local anaesthetics, which of the statements is not true?
options: Smaller Diameter Fibers are more susceptible than Larger Diameters Fibers.
C Fibers are more susceptible than B Fibers
Myelinated Fibers are more susceptible than Unmyelinated Fibers.
Delta Fibers are more susceptible than A Beta Fibers.

explanation.short: B. C Fibers are more susceptible than B Fibers
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. C Fibers are more susceptible than B Fibers

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Order of Susceptibility of Nerve Fibers to Local Anesthetics
  • • Order of Susceptibility of Nerve Fibers to Local Anesthetics
  • • Option A . Smaller Fibers>>> susceptible than Larger Fibers
  • • Option A
  • • Option C . Myelinated Fibers are more susceptible than unmyelinated fibers
  • • Option C
  • • Option D . B > A Gamma> A Delta> A Beta> A Alpha> C Fibers
  • • Option D

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective
  • ➤ The order of susceptibility of nerve fibers to local anesthetics is as follows: B Fibers > A Gamma Fibers > A Delta Fibers > A Beta Fibers > A Alpha Fibers > C fibers, with smaller diameter fibers generally being more susceptible than larger diameter fibers and myelinated fibers being more susceptible than unmyelinated fibers.

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 35 years old female patient has a BMI of 36 kg/m 2 . She has multiple comorbidities and hence presented to the bariatric surgeon for surgical options to lose weight. Which of the below parameters will make her unfit for surgery?
options: Her BMI is below the Cut off for Surgery

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Severe Depression

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Let's evaluate all the options to identify which parameter would make a 35-year-old female patient with a BMI of 36 kg/m^2 unfit for bariatric surgery:
  • • Option A. Coronary artery disease is a significant comorbidity that needs to be well-managed before bariatric surgery, but it does not necessarily make a patient unfit for surgery if optimized. In fact, it is one of the co-morbidities which can be considered as an indication .
  • • Option A.
  • • significant comorbidity
  • • well-managed
  • • indication
  • • Option B . A BMI below the cut-off for surgery could make a patient ineligible for bariatric surgery. However, a BMI of 36 kg/m^2 is above the typical cut-off of 35 kg/m^2 with comorbidities or 40 kg/m^2 without comorbidities.
  • • Option B
  • • patient ineligible
  • • Option C . Being capable of understanding lifestyle changes is a necessary criterion for suitability for bariatric surgery, not a disqualifying one.
  • • Option C
  • • understanding lifestyle changes

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Severe depression can make a patient unfit for bariatric surgery because it may affect their ability to understand and comply with the significant lifestyle changes required postoperatively .
  • ➤ patient unfit

  • ➤ comply with

  • ➤ required postoperatively

question: A 45 years old lady underwent Roux-en-Y gastric bypass 2 years ago. She comes to the emergency with sudden onset abdominal pain and vomiting. She is afebrile. Abdomen exam shows vague diffuse tenderness. An urgent CECT abdomen is done, which shows dilated small bowel loops. Which of these is a dangerous late complication in this case?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Internal Hernia

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . Narrowing of gastro-jejunostomy can cause obstruction and vomiting but would typically not lead to diffuse abdominal tenderness and is more of an early complication post-surgery.
  • • Option A

  • • vomiting
  • • Option C. A leak from the staple line is generally an early postoperative complication and would typically present with signs of infection such as fever , which is not mentioned in this scenario.

  • • early postoperative complication
  • • present with signs of infection
  • • fever
  • • Option D. Pulmonary embolism could cause sudden onset symptoms but would not explain the finding of dilated small bowel loops on the CECT abdomen.
  • • Option D.
  • • sudden onset symptoms

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ An internal hernia is a dangerous late complication after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass that can present with symptoms of bowel obstruction and requires high priority for laparoscopic investigation , even in the face of normal imaging studies .

CT scanning has a high rate of false negatives for internal hernia , so anyone presenting with severe , cramping abdominal pain 2–3 years after surgery needs to be high priority for investigation by laparoscopy . Closure of the internal hernia spaces is now standard of care in Roux-en-Y gastric bypass.

question: Masaoka staging is used to classify:

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Thymoma

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ The Masaoka staging system is used to classify thymoma , assessing the degree of tumor invasion to guide treatment, which commonly includes thymectomy .
  • ➤ Masaoka staging system
  • ➤ classify thymoma
  • ➤ degree of tumor invasion
  • ➤ thymectomy
  • ➤ This is the most common anterior mediastinal tumour , accounting for 25% of the total , and is derived from the thymus gland . Thymomas vary in behaviour from benign to aggressively invasive, as classified in the Masoaka classification system used to stage thymomas and more recently the TNM classification. They are often related to myasthenia gravis , a neuromuscular condition that can have a high associated incidence of thymomas , and interestingly may respond to excision of the thymus gland even when the gland has no associated thymoma present. The only reliable indicator of malignancy is capsular invasion . Diagnosis and treatment are best achieved by complete thymectomy , which for large tumours (>5 cm) Treatment - Thymectomy : Open surgery / video assisted thoracoscopic surgery ( VATS )

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 55-year-old man presents to the emergency room with severe chest pain and difficulty swallowing. A CT scan with water soluble contrast reveals an esophageal perforation. What is the most common etiology of esophageal perforation?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Instrumental

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A: Spontaneous. Spontaneous perforation of the esophagus , also known as Boerhaave syndrome , is a serious condition but not the most common cause . It occurs due to vomiting against a closed glottis , which leads to a sudden increase in esophageal pressure and typically results in perforation of the lower esophagus.
  • • Option A: Spontaneous.
  • • Spontaneous perforation
  • • esophagus
  • • Boerhaave syndrome
  • • serious condition but not the most common cause
  • • due to vomiting against a closed glottis
  • • increase in esophageal pressure
  • • perforation of the lower esophagus.
  • • Option B: Blunt trauma to the chest. Blunt trauma can cause esophageal perforation ; however, it is less common than iatrogenic causes related to medical procedures.
  • • Option B: Blunt trauma to the chest.
  • • cause esophageal perforation
  • • less common than iatrogenic
  • • Option D: Penetrating trauma to chest. Penetrating trauma is another possible cause of esophageal injury , but again, it is not as common as iatrogenic causes.
  • • Option D: Penetrating trauma to chest.
  • • another possible cause of esophageal injury

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The most common cause of esophageal perforation is iatrogenic injury during instrumental procedures such as upper GI endoscopy, dilatation, and stenting .
  • ➤ most common cause of esophageal perforation
  • ➤ iatrogenic injury
  • ➤ instrumental procedures
  • ➤ upper GI endoscopy, dilatation, and stenting
  • ➤ Iatrogenic perforation secondary to endoscopic procedures such as dilatation of strictures or achalasia is the most common cause. Esophageal perforation can occur due to instrumental procedures such as upper GI endoscopy , dilatation, and stenting. Spontaneous esophageal perforation , also known as Boerhaave syndrome , can occur when vomiting against a closed glottis , resulting in a massive increase in esophageal pressure and typically perforating the lower esophagus over the left pleura. CT with water-soluble contrast is the ideal imaging modality to diagnose esophageal perforation. Treatment options for esophageal perforation include conservative management for small perforations and surgery for septic load (Boerhaave syndrome), unstable mediastinitis, or intra-abdominal esophageal perforation.
  • ➤ Iatrogenic perforation secondary to endoscopic procedures such as dilatation of strictures or achalasia is the most common cause.
  • ➤ Iatrogenic perforation
  • ➤ most common cause.
  • ➤ Esophageal perforation can occur due to instrumental procedures such as upper GI endoscopy , dilatation, and stenting.
  • ➤ Esophageal perforation

  • ➤ Spontaneous esophageal perforation , also known as Boerhaave syndrome , can occur when vomiting against a closed glottis , resulting in a massive increase in esophageal pressure and typically perforating the lower esophagus over the left pleura.
  • ➤ Spontaneous esophageal perforation
  • ➤ Boerhaave syndrome
  • ➤ vomiting against a closed glottis

The size and extent of the perforation will determine the treatment approach. Small perforations may be managed conservatively, while larger perforations may require surgical exploration.
  • • Option B. Scope induced:
  • • induced by an upper GI endoscopy, dilatation, or stenting
  • • Option D. Oesophageal cancer can perforate , and the prognosis is usually poor since it reflects the underlying advanced disease.

  • • perforate

It can occur when vomiting against a closed glottis, resulting in a massive increase in esophageal pressure and typically perforating the lower esophagus over the left pleura.

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which type of caustic injury to esophagus causes deeper tissue damage?
options: Both acid and alkali poisons cause the same extent of damage
Caustic injury does not cause tissue damage

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Alkali poisons

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Acid poisons cause coagulative necrosis , which results in superficial tissue damage . The extent of trauma is generally less than that caused by alkali poisons.
  • • Option A.
  • • Acid poisons cause coagulative necrosis
  • • superficial tissue damage
  • • Option C. Acid and alkali poisons cause different types of tissue damage, with alkali poisons causing deeper damage than acid poisons .
  • • Option C.
  • • alkali poisons causing deeper damage than acid poisons
  • • Option D. Caustic injury always causes tissue damage and can lead to scarring and strictures in the esophagus, stomach, and duodenum. It is a premalignant condition for esophageal carcinoma.
  • • Option D.
  • • Caustic injury
  • • scarring and strictures
  • • premalignant condition
  • • Therefore, the correct answer is B) Alkali poisons cause deeper tissue damage than acid poisons.

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Alkali poisons cause deeper tissue damage to the esophagus compared to acid poisons due to their mechanism of causing liquefactive necrosis , which allows for deeper penetration and more extensive injury .
  • ➤ Alkali poisons
  • ➤ deeper tissue damage to the esophagus
  • ➤ acid poisons due to their mechanism of causing liquefactive necrosis
  • ➤ deeper penetration and more extensive injury

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 5-year-old child accidentally swallowed a foreign object while playing. What is the most dangerous foreign object that a child can swallow?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Battery

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A: Food bolus . A food bolus can cause obstruction but is typically not as dangerous as other objects because it often softens and passes through the gastrointestinal tract or can be removed endoscopically without causing significant damage.
  • • Option A: Food bolus
  • • obstruction
  • • not as dangerous as other objects because it often softens and passes through the gastrointestinal tract
  • • Option B: Fish Bone . A fish bone can be sharp and potentially puncture the esophagus or other parts of the gastrointestinal tract . However, it is generally less harmful compared to corrosive or electrically charged objects because it does not cause tissue necrosis or systemic toxicity.
  • • Option B: Fish Bone
  • • sharp and potentially puncture the esophagus
  • • gastrointestinal tract
  • • less harmful compared to corrosive or electrically charged objects
  • • does not cause tissue necrosis
  • • Option D: Coin. A coin can become lodged in the esophagus , particularly at one of the natural constrictions , but it generally does not cause the same degree of tissue damage as a battery . The coin can often be retrieved endoscopically without significant complication.
  • • Option D: Coin.
  • • lodged in the esophagus
  • • one of the natural constrictions
  • • does not cause the same degree of tissue damage as a battery

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The most dangerous foreign object that a child can swallow is a battery due to the risk of electrical discharge , leakage of caustic substances, and the potential for rapid tissue damage and necrosis.
  • ➤ most dangerous foreign object
  • ➤ child can swallow is a battery
  • ➤ risk of electrical discharge
  • ➤ Swallowing a foreign object can be a serious medical emergency, especially in children. The most common foreign objects that are swallowed are food boluses and bones . However, the most dangerous foreign object that a child can swallow is a battery. The esophagus has several constrictions where foreign objects can become lodged. The three most common locations for foreign body impaction are at the cricopharynx (15cm from the incisors), at the left bronchus crossing the esophagus (25cm from the incisors), and at the diaphragmatic pleura (40cm from the incisors). The first step in managing a swallowed foreign body is to obtain a chest X-ray to determine the location of the object. If the object is in the esophagus or stomach, endoscopic retrieval may be possible . However, if the object is a battery, it must be removed as an emergency via endoscopy or colonoscopy , depending on the location of the foreign body.
  • ➤ Swallowing a foreign object can be a serious medical emergency, especially in children. The most common foreign objects that are swallowed are food boluses and bones . However, the most dangerous foreign object that a child can swallow is a battery.

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 30-year-old female presents with colicky abdominal pain and bilious vomiting. She reports weight loss following a keto-diet in the past few months. On investigation, the small bowel appears normal, but a CECT of the abdomen reveals compression of the D3 segment of the duodenum due to narrowing of the angle of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). Which of the following is the best option for treatment of this condition?
options: Administration of medications to relieve pain
Feeding the patient to promote weight gain
Endoscopic intervention to widen the narrowed angle

explanation.short: C. Feeding the patient to promote weight gain
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Feeding the patient to promote weight gain

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A: Surgery to remove the obstruction . Surgical intervention may be considered if conservative measures fail . It is not the first line of treatment because the syndrome is often caused by a lack of mesenteric fat padding, not an actual physical obstruction that needs to be removed.
  • • Option A: Surgery to remove the obstruction
  • • Surgical intervention
  • • conservative measures fail
  • • not the first line of treatment
  • • Option B: Administration of medications to relieve pain . While pain management is an important aspect of patient care , medications alone will not address the underlying issue of the narrowed angle between the SMA and the aorta, which is causing the duodenal compression.
  • • Option B: Administration of medications to relieve pain
  • • pain management is an important aspect of patient care
  • • medications alone will not address the underlying issue
  • • Option D: Endoscopic intervention to widen the narrowed angle . Endoscopic intervention is not the standard treatment for SMA Syndrome because the problem is not within the duodenal lumen but rather external compression.
  • • Option D: Endoscopic intervention to widen the narrowed angle
  • • Endoscopic intervention
  • • not the standard treatment for SMA Syndrome

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The initial treatment for SMA Syndrome is conservative , focusing on nutritional support to promote weight gain and increase the mesenteric fat pad, thereby relieving the compression of the duodenum.
  • ➤ initial treatment for SMA Syndrome is conservative
  • ➤ SMA Syndrome , also known as Wilkie Syndrome , is a rare cause of intestinal obstruction . It occurs due to the narrowing of the angle between the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and aorta, leading to compression of the third part of the duodenum (D3) .
  • ➤ SMA Syndrome , also known as Wilkie Syndrome , is a rare cause of intestinal obstruction . It occurs due to the narrowing of the angle between the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and aorta, leading to compression of the third part of the duodenum (D3) .
  • ➤ SMA Syndrome
  • ➤ Wilkie Syndrome
  • ➤ rare cause of intestinal obstruction
  • ➤ compression of the third part of the duodenum (D3)
  • ➤ Risk factors:
  • ➤ Risk factors:
  • ➤ Sudden weight loss Anorexia nervosa Prolonged bed rest Scoliosis The clinical features of SMA Syndrome include colicky abdominal pain, bilious vomiting, and weight loss. On investigation, the small bowel appears normal, but a CECT of the abdomen may reveal compression of the D3 segment of the duodenum due to narrowing of the angle of the SMA. The initial treatment for SMA Syndrome is conservative and involves feeding the patient to promote weight gain and open the narrowed angle. This can be achieved through the placement of a naso-jejunostomy tube. If this fails, surgery may be required to bypass the duodenum and relieve the obstruction.

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 50-year-old male patient with a history of peptic ulcer disease presents with a complaint of non-bilious vomiting. On examination, a succussion splash is heard over abdomen. CECT reveals the presence of a pyloric stricture. Which of the following is the first step for management of this condition?
options: Conservative management with nasogastric decompression and washout
Surgical reconstruction using Billroth II procedure
Mikulicz stricturoplasty to relieve the obstruction

explanation.short: A. Conservative management with nasogastric decompression and washout
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Conservative management with nasogastric decompression and washout

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option B: Surgical reconstruction using Billroth II procedure . The Billroth II procedure is a surgical option (gastro-jejunostomy) that is generally considered after conservative management has failed.
  • • Option B: Surgical reconstruction using Billroth II procedure
  • • surgical option
  • • generally considered after conservative management has failed.
  • • Option C: Endoscopic dilatation of the stricture . Endoscopic dilatation is a less invasive treatment option compared to surgery and can be effective in managing strictures . However, it is not the first-line treatment before attempting nasogastric decompression.
  • • Option C: Endoscopic dilatation of the stricture
  • • less invasive treatment option compared to surgery
  • • effective in managing strictures
  • • Option D: Mikulicz strictureplasty to relieve the obstruction . Mikulicz strictureplasty is a surgical technique used when strictures are not amenable to endoscopic dilatation . It is considered a more advanced step if other less invasive measures fail.
  • • Option D: Mikulicz strictureplasty to relieve the obstruction
  • • surgical technique used when strictures are not amenable to endoscopic dilatation

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The first step in managing a pyloric stricture is conservative management with nasogastric decompression and washout to provide symptomatic relief
  • ➤ first step in managing a pyloric stricture
  • ➤ conservative management
  • ➤ nasogastric decompression
  • ➤ Pyloric stricture is a late complication of peptic ulcer disease , which occurs due to the fibrosis and scarring that develop when the circumferential ulcers over the stomach heal. This can lead to narrowing of the stomach or gastric outlet obstruction, which usually occurs at the first part of the duodenum. Clinical features of pyloric stricture include a history of peptic ulcer disease and non-bilious vomiting . The management of pyloric stricture depends on the extent and severity of the obstruction . Initial conservative management involves nasogastric decompression and washout , which can provide symptomatic relief. Endoscopy can be used to confirm the presence of a stricture and to obtain a biopsy to rule out adenocarcinoma . If the stricture is benign, it can be treated with dilatation. If dilatation fails, surgery may be required to relieve the obstruction. Mikulicz stricturoplasty is a surgical procedure that involves opening the pylorus horizontally and closing it vertically to relieve the obstruction . This is a preferred option when the stricture is not amenable to endoscopic dilatation. Billroth II reconstruction is another surgical option that involves removal of the pylorus and reconstruction of the gastrointestinal tract to improve gastric emptying.
  • ➤ Pyloric stricture is a late complication of peptic ulcer disease , which occurs due to the fibrosis and scarring that develop when the circumferential ulcers over the stomach heal. This can lead to narrowing of the stomach or gastric outlet obstruction, which usually occurs at the first part of the duodenum.

question: A 60-year-old male, a known case of chronic atrial fibrillation presents with acute abdominal pain and PR bleed. Abdominal examination revealed vague tenderness and diminished bowel sounds. Which of the following is the investigation of choice for diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) CECT abdomen angiogram

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A: Serum lipase. While serum lipase can be useful in diagnosing pancreatitis , it would not be the most helpful initial test for a patient suspected of having mesenteric ischemia.
  • • Option A: Serum lipase.
  • • serum lipase can be useful in diagnosing pancreatitis
  • • Option B: USG abdomen. An ultrasound of the abdomen can be useful for assessing many abdominal conditions, but it may not be the best choice for evaluating blood flow or identifying mesenteric ischemia, especially in an acute setting.
  • • Option B: USG abdomen.
  • • ultrasound of the abdomen can be useful for assessing many abdominal conditions,
  • • Option C: X-ray upper abdomen erect. An erect abdominal X-ray can show signs of bowel obstruction or free air under the diaphragm in cases of perforation but is not the most sensitive or specific test for mesenteric ischemia.
  • • Option C: X-ray upper abdomen erect.
  • • erect abdominal X-ray
  • • show signs of bowel obstruction or free air under the diaphragm

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ For a patient with suspected acute mesenteric ischemia , particularly in the context of chronic atrial fibrillation , the next best investigation is a CECT abdomen angiogram.
  • ➤ suspected acute mesenteric ischemia
  • ➤ context of chronic atrial fibrillation
  • ➤ next best investigation is a CECT abdomen angiogram.
  • ➤ SMA emboli may be carried from the left atrium in atrial fibrillation , the left ventricle after mural myocardial infarction , vegetations on mitral and aortic valves associated with endocarditis or an atheromatous plaque from an aortic aneurysm Investigation for mesenteric ischemia includes a CECT scan, which can determine whether the occlusion is acute or chronic and assess bowel viability or gangrene. Treatment for mesenteric ischemia depends on the extent and severity of the obstruction. Conservative management with antibiotics and bowel rest may be used in some cases. For acute occlusion, mesenteric vascular intervention is the preferred treatment . If there is evidence of gangrene, laparotomy and bowel resection may be required. Chronic mesenteric ischemia typically presents with mesenteric angina (central abdominal pain) and diarrhoea. A CECT angiogram can reveal atherosclerosis of the mesentery. Treatment for chronic mesenteric ischemia involves mesenteric angioplasty bypass.

Antibiotics, enteral nutrition, MRI, and surgical intervention
Conservative management, low-residue diet, ultrasound, and outpatient follow-up
Bowel rest, antibiotics, barium enema, and observation

explanation.detail:

Explanation:

  • • management

  • • not be feasible

  • • Option C: Conservative management, low-residue diet, ultrasound, and outpatient follow-up
  • • Conservative management
  • • indicated in some cases
  • • Option D: Bowel rest, antibiotics, barium enema, and observation . Bowel rest and observation are part of the initial management , but merely antibiotics might not be sufficient for sepsis control , and a barium enema is not typically used for fistula assessment due to the risk of extravasation and worsening sepsis.

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The management protocol for enterocutaneous fistula involves sepsis control, skin care, total parenteral nutrition (TPN), anatomical delineation with a CECT scan , and planned surgical repair following the SNAP protocol.
  • ➤ management protocol for enterocutaneous fistula
  • ➤ sepsis control, skin care, total parenteral nutrition
  • ➤ anatomical delineation with a CECT scan
  • ➤ planned surgical repair
  • ➤ Enterocutaneous fistula is an abnormal communication between the bowel and the skin, which can be caused by postoperative complications, Crohn's disease, GI malignancies, or radiation enteritis . The management of enterocutaneous fistula is based on the SNAP protocol . Sepsis control and skin care are essential for the management of enterocutaneous fistula, along with total parenteral nutrition (TPN) to provide adequate nutrition. Anatomical delineation, typically through a CECT scan, is necessary to identify the extent of the fistula and plan for repair. Planned repair is the ultimate goal of management, but high-output fistulas (>500ml) may not close spontaneously , and additional interventions may be required. Conservative management, a low-residue diet, and ultrasound are not effective for managing enterocutaneous fistula, and surgical intervention is required for definitive treatment.

  • ➤ Trichobezoar (hair balls) are unusual and are virtually exclusively found in young psychiatric patients. It is caused by the ingestion of hair, which remains undigested in the stomach. The hair ball can lead to ulceration and gastrointestinal bleeding, perforation or obstruction . The diagnosis is made easily at endoscopy. Treatment consists of removal of the bezoar, which may require open surgical treatment. Phytobezoars are made of vegetable matter and are found principally in patients who have gastric stasis, usually following gastric surgery.

explanation.detail:

Explanation:

  • ➤ Superior mesenteric artery (SMA) syndrome , also known as Wilkie syndrome is a rare acquired vascular compression disorder in which acute angulation of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) results in compression of the third part of the duodenum, leading to obstruction. Patients with SMA syndrome may present acutely, with chronic insidious symptomatology, or with an acute exacerbation of chronic symptoms: Acute presentation is usually characterised by signs and symptoms of duodenal obstruction Chronic cases may present with long-standing vague abdominal symptoms, early satiety and anorexia, or recurrent episodes of abdominal pain, associated with vomiting.
